title: Chris Watling on AI, inflation, oil and the fragile foundations beneath markets

segments.text: On this episode of the Rules of Investing Podcast, I speak with Chris Wattling, CEO and
Chief Market Strategist at Longview Economics.
When we spoke a couple of days ago, we covered the outlook for oil prices and inflation,
the issues he thinks matter most for investors right now, and the equity markets that he
thinks offer the best opportunities around the world on a 2-3 year view.
Speak around for his take on Australia's favourite dinner table conversation, Australian
Residential Housing.
I'm Patrick Poke, and this podcast is brought to you by Live Wire Markets.
Chris, lovely to be chatting with you again.
It's been a while since we've been on a video together, very keen to hear some of your
views, but I think before we get into the minutia, although definitely big picture is your
story.
Let's start off really broad.
There's a lot that investors are kind of juggling at the moment in terms of competing narratives,
geopolitics, inflation, rates, AI, slowing growth.
There really is a lot going on in the world.
From your perspective, what actually matters the most right now?
Yeah, there is a lot going on, isn't it?
I think you've really boiled it down into three key trends.
Obviously, energy and the Straits of Hormuz, which we'll get on to in more detail.
I'm sure it's a critical question.
Number one, number two is the AI trend undoubtedly.
I mean, we've seen what that's been doing with a bunch of stocks around the world, really.
They call it the switch from GPU to CPU.
Now we've got to genticate AI at work and CPU so important.
And then thirdly, I think is the idea that the global, the Western economy was starting
to broaden as we got into the back end of last year.
Can we get back to that?
I think that's a critical question.
Is that going to be derailed by what's happening with the oil price?
And all those kind of factors, and then you can lay on top of that geopolitics, midterms,
Trump meeting Z, Z meeting Putin, there's an awful lot to think about.
It's a fascinating time, as it always is.
Let's jump into the oil situation and what's happening in Iran.
How are you thinking about the medium and the long-term implications, both from like an
economic perspective, but also from a market's perspective?
Yeah, look, I mean, we've been crunching numbers trying to work out what oil price causes
what damage wear and who's in the most trouble.
I think the markets come to that view quite well.
It's pretty well understood that Europe's more vulnerable of the major economic regions
of the world, $100 oil price pushes Europe, I think, into a technical recession.
I'm not sure why I should worry too much about that.
I think one can exaggerate the importance of that, but that's certainly out there.
The US, I think, is resilient, until around $1,7200 oil.
It's a big oil export, as we know.
This is very different from the 1970s oil intensity of GDP in the US is fallen dramatically
in the last few decades.
They still use about $21 million per day, which is what they were doing about 20 years
ago.
US is more resilient, and China's built up this enormous stockpile.
All of that's a big focus.
I think then beyond that, really, what I'm thinking about when you talk about markets,
obviously, once concerned about the lack of physical oil, the risk of inventory is running
down, and there's a big debate out there as when jet fuel is going to run down, when
the inventory is going to run out, when they get to critical levels, that is clearly
important.
I think almost more important is what's happening to six and 12-month oil price.
If you look at the six, 12-month futures, what are they up to?
They're really telling you about where we're going to be, where we come out of this crisis
at some point, and they're really telling you about, I think, the price of oil that's
needed to get enough oil out of the ground to replace a lot of what you see coming out
of Iran and other parts of the Straits of Hormuz.
What I like about these kind of events is people adapt, markets adapt, people adapt,
economies adapt, a lot of oil is going through the East-West pipeline in Saudi, UAE is talking
about building a second pipeline to the Fruja port, clearly $90 oil, which is roughly
where six-month Brent futures are.
It's high enough to get Shell going again, it gets more Canadian oil out, it gets Brazilian
deep water oil out, hopefully we'll get Venezuela going more.
Interestingly, before we came into this, there was a lot of oil in the world.
There was plenty of oil around.
The prices are doubled, let's say, on Spartan, up 50% on six-month forward.
That's quite a lot in terms of getting even more oil out of the world.
We're focused on those economic factors and we're focused on what's happening to six-month
future Brent contract, because I think that is the place to watch if you want to think
about the impact on global stock markets.
That is the good way of understanding.
It sort of brings together all that's happening in the world of geopolitics.
As I say to investors in the last few weeks, good luck working out what Iranian intentions
are, because I'm pretty sure there's no one in the West that really knows.
Yeah, or Trump for that matter.
Well, you know, in a sense, you're right on Trump, but we do know what Trump wants
an offeramp.
You know what I mean?
I think it's quite clear he wants an offeramp, so it's actually Iran that we don't really
know.
There's a lot of theories, but we don't really know.
You mentioned a lot of oil supply coming online from places like Brazil and Canada,
and I think you mentioned Venezuela as well.
There's obviously, you know, there's an incentive price there, right, and it's going
to vary depending on what the price is at the moment and how much it costs to pull that
out of the ground.
Do you think the US has really tapped out in their capacity at the moment?
Do you see that additional capacity is mainly coming from overseas, or is there still room
like the US is producing a lot these days?
They are producing a lot.
And they're sort of net net exporters of, I think it's three million barrels of petroleum
and crude.
And their net imports of crude, they're historically import crude to refine it into gasoline.
They need certain types of crude.
They tend to get from Canada and Venezuela plays like that.
Those net imports have actually gone back to zero.
So they're exporting crude more than they were, and they're exporting products more than
they were, you know, jet fuel and so on.
So, you know, what you can see in the data quite clearly, and we've been looking at this
over the last few days, is the inventories in the US are running down.
And there is a limit, obviously, to how much they can do that.
But I think, I mean, I think the story's about, you know, people talk about the straits
of hall moves being closed, 20% of the world's oil supply not getting through.
I think that's exaggerated.
And you really dig into the detail of that 20%, 15 million is crude, five is products.
And of that sort of 15, it looks to us like two or three is not getting through.
Other stuff is getting out different ways.
So, and oh, there's a bit of demand destruction, that's true.
Or there's a bit of relief from releasing inventories from the OECD supplies and so on.
So, you know, the world is feeling the pinch, but at the moment, the pinch is being made
up, mainly, really, by what you're seeing in the States.
So, yeah, I mean, there's a crunch point, there's definitely a crunch point.
It's difficult to work out exactly when it is, because there's so many moving parts.
And actually, the data's not quite as good as you would imagine outside the US.
But, yeah, I mean, the crunch point is, I guess, one to three months away.
Wow.
Okay.
That is, that's not very far.
Of course, that obvious flow on from oil is inflation.
It's for a lot of places, one of the biggest inputs to overall inflation for consumers
as well as producers.
I think a lot of investors really kind of thought, before all this happened, that inflation
was yesterday's problem.
Do you think that markets might still be underestimating the inflation risk, particularly if those
energy prices do stay elevated?
No, I don't think they are.
I think it's quite the reverse.
I think we're a risk of most central bankers around the world fighting yesterday's battle,
which is kind of often the pattern.
It's almost human nature, isn't it?
You worry most about what's just happened to you.
It's the thing that's most front and center of your mind.
And of course, central bankers missed the transitory inflation story of 2022-23.
They got that wrong.
I think they're kind of in that mindset when I really look, I mean, I'm not sure about
Australia.
I think when we'll get on to Australia, but it seems to be in a slightly different economic
place to most of the West.
But certainly, if I look at the UK, US and Eurozone, these are weak economies that, you
know, labor markets deteriorating.
There's no house price inflation in the US, UK, most of Eurozone, unemployment rates
are going up.
There's no respect, capacity, there's lots of disinflationary trends.
And so I think when you have an oil price spike into that, it's more, I mean, you know,
on the headline numbers, it might create a bit of inflation, but ultimately it's disinflationary
and deflationary because you're just taking money out of people's pockets.
And if there's no tightness in the labor market, you're not going to get a sort of second-round
wage effect.
So I think central bankers are worrying too much about second-round effects.
And the market started a price in Hikes into some of those curves, which again, I think
would be an error.
So, I mean, we literally just this morning, we had a UK inflation, which surprised dramatically
to the downside.
Yesterday, we had UK employment, which showed contraction in payroll employees, basically
those people who are employed by a business rather than the self-employed, down by a hundred
thousand on the month, which is a big number for the UK.
So, yeah, I think inflation is really not the issue here.
The issue is weak economies and just taking out purchasing power of people's pockets.
Yes, certainly, anecdotally, on the ground here, we saw petrol prices spike to $2.50 or
even higher in some places immediately after the conflict started, but they're pretty
much back to where they started.
Maybe a little bit higher, but certainly, we're paying $1.70, $1.80 a liter now, which is
only marginally higher than it was in January and February.
Yeah, it's interesting.
I mean, again, I mean, if you look at UK petrol prices, similar sort of thing, they spiked
on the conflict, but they've been higher over the last 15 years, and they've held their
own for the last couple of months.
They haven't actually come off like you also.
But yeah, I think it's an important consideration.
It's very different from 2022.
Electricity prices in much of the West were up dramatically in 2022.
It's not the case.
If you look at natural gas prices in the UK, the Eurozone, and the US haven't really
up that much compared to 2022 and actually they've eased in recent months.
So I think the world's very different.
The reactions different, the global economies and different places we come into this
2022 happened after massive COVID money printing, a massive amounts of money in
people's bank accounts.
So when you've got a lot of money in your bank account and someone puts up the old price,
you don't care.
When you haven't got a lot of money in your bank account, some say, you get you get a lot
and you buy a lot less of something else, and that's the point.
This is ultimately, it's a tax on the consumer when you hike the old price.
It's like it was 10 years ago, and I think that's the way to think about it.
And I just hope central banks don't respond inappropriately and jack up rates too much.
If at all, I think most of the West shouldn't be raising rates at all, even cutting them
in some instances.
OK, that's really interesting.
Let's switch to talking a little bit closer to home for us, that is, not for you.
How different do you think the outlook is for inflation and for interest rates in Australia
compared to the US and Europe?
Well, I mean, I think one good way to judge it is just to look at the relative performance
of housing market.
So I always think housing is a good indicator of the sort of tightness of money, monetary
policy, how the economy is functioning and so on.
And if you look at the UK or the USA and contrast it with Europe, it's sorry, with Australia.
It's a real contrast.
Now look, as you know, I'm not following Australia's closely as I'd like to.
But I keep a sort of rough eye on it and you just pretty good bet under your housing market
by the looks of things prices are up approvals are up on building approvals.
There seems to be a bit of an upswing there.
If you look to the US, you look at housing activity, it's in the doldrums.
I mean, it's in the three-year slump housing transactions is pretty much a record
loads.
If you look to the Michigan, you know, to people on a buy house, they're like, no, thank
you, definitely not.
And they've never been, you know, never been less inclined since the early 1980s.
So, you know, so housing is telling us that the cycles are in different places.
The labour markets are telling us that as well with rising unemployment in much of Europe
and the US, whereas in, of course, in Australia, you're sort of edging up in some employment
gains.
I don't think it's dramatic, is it?
But so it's a different point in the cycle.
So you're getting different central bank responses, I guess, is the word to think about it.
I'm sure you probably don't follow our local politics particularly closely, but there's
been a lot of noise around about the changes to capital gains tax and to negative gearing
and these kind of things.
So it would be interesting to see whether that really does actually have the impact on demand
for housing that some people have speculated that it will.
I guess it's one of those time will tell kind of situations.
Yeah.
So I was going to say I love that debate.
I've heard that many times over the years coming to Australia and I'm looking forward
to being back there in a couple of months and hearing more on that one because, yeah, it
is unique to your wonderful country and unique to your housing market, of course, and
probably has quite significant distortion effects.
So fascinating.
Yeah.
It really is.
I'll be watching from the sidelines, no skin in that game.
Looking at global growth over the next few years, what are some of the big sources of strength
that you see out there and are there any major areas of concern for you?
Yes, and yes.
I mean, definitely areas of concern, but let's start with the strength as I sort of touched
on when we when we started to talk.
There was clear evidence of a broadening Western recovery as we came into this year.
So if you looked at the industrial cycle in the US, for example, it, you know, 2023,
24 into 25, it was pretty much flat as a pancake.
And late 2025, it started to pick up and you can see an ISM manufacturing and you can
see it in durable goods orders which are trending higher.
So there's clear evidence of broadening in the Western economies in terms of the industrial
cycle, European PMIs for the manufacturing sector, pushed above 50 in the last couple
of months.
And I think we're also starting to see a credit cycle or we were.
So the strength was the broadening that was coming through.
The interesting thing is how much this oil price spike impacts that and slows it down.
But I think that was a real positive that was out there.
But look, I mean, there's plenty to worry about.
People point to the US consumer data.
They point to the lack of employment growth in the states.
The slowing of personal disposable income growth, which is really running at levels consistent
with at least a mid cycle slowdown if not a recession in the US.
And, you know, there were some real challenges there that you can look at delinquency rates
in in auto's and credit cards in the US that are pretty high levels and they've been
going up.
So there's definitely a bifurcated US macro backdrop, which does create vulnerability,
which I think is very important to keep an eye on them.
You can flip that on its head and really think about it the other way around that actually
the US economy is really driven by a very narrow construct, which I think enhances the
fragility of what's going on.
And what do I mean by that?
Well, I mean that really consumption is carrying on growing, obviously not because the lower
income workers who are not getting jobs as we just discuss is not much personal disposable
income growth, consumption is carrying on because of the wealth effect.
Now the wealth effect, of course, is in the market because of the AI theme.
And of course, capex is strong because of the AI theme, owning growth in the stock market
is strong because of the AI theme.
And if you look at the margins in the stock market, the record highs, you could look at
US corporate profit show GDP, which is the highest it's ever been on record post World War
All of that is about the AI theme.
So AI and related sectors are really driving the whole US economic story, US stock market
and US profit growth.
So there's a real narrowness to what's going on.
And if you look outside of that, as I say, you look at US housing, look at US consumer,
look at the lower income levels, there's real stress or certainly a degree of stress and
or a lack of growth.
Let's put it that way.
So, so that that I think is the real vulnerability, whilst the AI theme continues, we don't need
to worry about it, but I think when that comes to a conclusion, if that broadening thesis
hasn't played out, there'll be a real vulnerability in the US economy.
So just as everything's built on the narrow AI construct, it can easily collapse on an
unwinding or a lack of belief or a change in that construct, whatever that might be.
So don't get me wrong, I'm not suggesting it's all over tomorrow, and there are ways of
keeping an eye on these things.
But it is a fragility that does concern me along along with the straights of one moves
as well, but particularly that AI construct, I think that's a major medium term issue.
Definitely an interesting one.
Let's, I guess, get away more from the, say, the theoretical, at least from an investor perspective.
I guess from a real economy perspective, it's not theoretical, but for investors, the question
really becomes like, how does that apply to equity markets?
So looking out across the world at the moment, if you had to pick one major equity
market that you think offers the best opportunity over the kind of medium to long term, where
would you be looking at the moment and why?
Well, what a great question.
It depends what one means by medium to long term, so...
All right, let's put a number on it.
Let's go, let's say over kind of two to three years, maybe go out to five if you're feeling
particularly confident, but I think, I think most people would call that kind of medium
to long term.
I think the US has got a year in it.
I don't know if it's got two or three, so US large caps, I'd be cautious on.
Whether it's six months, 18 months, 24 months, I'm not sure, but the signs will tell us
when that's drawing to a close.
And the benefit, the beneficiaries of that will be, you know, I think places like Southern
Europe.
I'm a big fan of Southern Europe.
We've been pushing it for a couple of years.
Something was one of the best performing stock markets in the world last year.
Spanish mid caps, as well as large caps are starting to do well.
And really, you know, I tend to like to think more in terms of sectors than countries,
because really, each country has a sector bias.
US is obviously growth, Italy is banks, you know, et cetera, et cetera.
So I think on a two to three year view, I want to go somewhere where the bank's heavy,
they're going to enjoy a steepening yield curve.
Because when this AI bubble reaches its apogee and starts to go the other way in terms of
the stock market, not necessarily in terms of the capex, but when the US stock market starts
to top out, I think you're going to find more rate cuts come into the curve and you're
going to get a steeping of the yield curve.
This is going to be great for banks and financials.
So, you know, that to me supports the Southern European trade, the fact that you're going
to two years out be having lower rates is going to support the Southern European trade.
I think all of that is quite good on a two to three year view.
So really, I think short term, you need a bit of exposure to tech and AI.
You need to be, you know, hold it lightly and watch for the indicators when it's getting
towards its peak.
And then on the flip side, you want exposure to cyclicals and I'm thinking more financials
and industrial cyclicals, which I think will do well over the course of the next two
or three years.
So it gives you those kind of countries, basically.
I mean, can give you quite a few other countries.
You can throw in some of the emerging markets as well into that mix, but I wouldn't do the
tech focus ones.
I would do you so you need to be slightly careful if you buy emerging markets as a basket
because there's a lot of different flavors in there.
Three in Taiwan, obviously tech, China's sort of bit of everything.
And then you get the sort of classic cyclical commodity plays, which the sort of Brazil,
Mexico, Chile, I mean, Chile is a great place to invest in because it's a copper play.
And I think copper has gone a long, long term uptrend that's been brewing for a while
and is now playing out.
So there's lots of country picks based on macro themes and sector leadership in the global
stock market that would lead me to those sort of places on that two to three or maybe
even five year time frame.
So we're coming to the end.
The point is we're coming to the end of this long US bull market.
The question is, you know, we're late cycle, not ender cycle in the US.
And when that happens, what are the winners elsewhere in the world on the back of that?
And I think it's some of those countries I just mentioned.
Yeah.
Chile, definitely interesting from an equity perspective.
I'm not sure if I'd invest in their government bonds, but no, that's not what you're suggesting.
No, you may have actually already answered the question there, I think, but I'd still
be curious just to put it out there on the flip slide to that.
Are there any particular markets or popular trades that you think concern that you're concerned
about the risk over the longer term or medium to long term?
Well, who the US is one, I'm concerned also about the UK, because of the way our politics
is going, we're in the depths of a new leader about to be appointed, and our Prime Minister
are probably about to be deposed by his own party, the Labour party.
So again, yeah, well, it's not, it's not again in the Labour party terms, but it's again
in terms of Britain, you're right, the Italians, so we're apparently joking the other
day that we've had more Prime Ministers than anyone else in the world in the last 10
years, which is, you know when the Italians say that, you're in trouble, they've definitely
got the record post World War II, but it seems like we've got the last 10 years record
on the nose.
I mean, I remember I've been coming to Australia for a long time, so your Prime Minister
seems to change a lot as well, but not so much because of infighting more, just because
you have a short political cycle, but yeah, we've had a lot of leaders in the last
decade, and it's not going so well.
Well, let's get on to everybody's favourite topic of conversation over the dinner table
in Australia at least.
We referred to it a little bit earlier, sometimes known as Australia's National Religion, Australian
Residential Property.
Look, I know this isn't really your area, so I guess we'd probably say, let's take
this one with a grain of salt, but talking to an economist who's focused on markets,
it would be remiss of me not to ask you about Australian Residential Property.
So what is your view there?
What do you think the outlook will be particularly with affordability, looking stretched, rates
still relatively high, and of course the recent, recently announced, if not yet enacted,
changes to the tax treatment.
Well, if it changes the tax treatment of what you said, that sounds pretty troubling for
the housing market, but I have to say obviously I haven't really studied them, and I'm famed
in Australia for calling the end of the housing market too soon, which I think many people
are.
I mean, I've had a good, I've had a good, not only one, but yeah, it's a crowded field.
I've had a good model in the housing markets around the world, but not in Australia, which
I seem to be defined gravity compared to the way the rest of the world behaves.
And I know people talk about immigration in Australia, and of course you have the negative
gearing and all that kind of stuff, which does make it, particularly negative gearing,
makes it very unique for investors there.
So you can see why perhaps it's been slightly different, but I, so I think it may be, if
that's the reason that Australia's held up so well compared to other places, perhaps
if you take away some of that, you start to cause the housing market quite a bit of trouble,
because as you know, and everyone in Australia knows, housing is absurdly expensive, and
deeply unaffordable for first-time buyers and it's a market, like many markets around
the world, but particularly in Australia, that's defined gravity.
So, and of course you have an RBA that's been raising rates now, and so I think it's a
troubling mix.
If they're raising rates into these tax changes, I say I don't really understand how
I haven't been following the details, so, but that would make me nervous in such an expensive
housing market.
So all eyes on that, but take it with a pinch of salt, because like I said, you know,
I've tried this one before, it didn't quite work.
Yeah, I think both overseas and Australian economists, whether they specialize in housing
or not have struggled with this one, but it definitely seems if the changes do go ahead
like it could be possibly the most significant thing to happen to housing in a long time.
But I also would not feel confident in thinking that it's all over, it's a tough one.
Yeah, difficult one.
Yeah.
You may know, as you've been on the show before, my favourite part of the show is our three
favourite questions that we ask everyone.
Now, I've put a little bit of a different spin on them for you as obviously you're not
a stock picker, so it seemed wrong, but we'll get to that one last.
First of the questions is over your career, can you think of a big call, either right
or wrong, that really shaped the way you think about investing today?
Plenty on both sides, we've been running this business out for 23 years and I was in
the city for 10 years before that, so you'd hope what you learned something over that
timeframe.
I mean, my funniest, most famous, well not any of them are favours, but my well-known
failed call was really in Covid, when I was on Bloomberg TV, I think in probably early
March 2020, suggesting it wasn't going to be much worse than the flu, and the lady practically
fell off her chair, who was hosting the programme at the time, but so I think we got that
spectacular wrong, which was really a lack of understanding about the importance of pandemics
the time that we had, hopefully that's something we've remedied, but I mean, I would take
the argument that markets primarily sell off for three reasons, that's really what we've
learned over the last 35 years, and number one is liquidity, liquidity, liquidity, which
is just so important, interest rates, tightness of money, tightness of monetary conditions,
tightness of financial conditions, utterly key, I think that's critical, number two is
in anticipation of recessions or mid-cycle slowdowns, so understanding the economic mini-cycles,
two to three years, and understanding the big recession drivers are key to forecasting
stock markets, and then number three is shocks, which of course is where Covid gets planted,
but as do major wars and as do liberation day announcements, but the other thing I've learned
is that markets almost always sell off, whether it's a shock or recession, etc, they almost,
that sell off almost always starts when markets, ingredient, market participants have overdone
it on the upside, and it sort of creates this one way facing global investment portfolio
pretty much, I mean, I'm exaggerating, but you get the drift, that's very vulnerable to
these sort of events, so fear and greed is highly important input, I think,
into forecasting markets on a multi-month basis, and also understanding major tops and
major lows, so macro risk appetite, these things are critical, and I think we've learned
that, and yeah, but look, after 25 years, plenty of calls that are wrong, on average though
we tend to be more right than wrong, which I think is the most important thing, and we do
track that, we actually publish our own track record to demonstrate that of all the calls
that we make, so, but yeah, tons of, tons of learning from mistakes, so important.
I think, I think the other thing to say is the crowd, the crowd is normally wrong, I mean,
I think we all, we would all agree with that, you know, and the more the crowd is screaming,
I love the narrative in markets, that major tops or major lows, everyone's, it's about
like gold in January, you know, everyone was convinced that gold was, you know, to the
moon to quote Elon Musk, it's kind of the moon, but of course that, that, you know,
when everyone's saying that, that's when you've got tops forming, in the medium term or
the long term, and I, you know, I think that's probably pretty major top we had in gold
on January the 29th, same in silver, so, so yeah, all this kind of stuff I think is critical,
understanding the economic cycle, the risk appetite, fear and greed cycle, the liquidity
cycle, and the many a maxi cycle, so all of that I think is critical.
The second of our favourite questions, what do you think investors are misunderstanding
or getting wrong in markets at the moment?
I think investors are a little bit too complacent about, about the construct of your economic
growth as we talked about earlier, and there's a lot of sort of belief in this AI theme,
perhaps not enough to make a major top, but I suspect that's probably in the most important
asset allocation call for any global investor over the next three to five years, so, you
know, I think, I think that's key, I think that's critical, and I think that's very, very
important to understand, and I think understanding what Trump's policies are doing to globalisation
and de-globalisation and the shift of multilateralism, understanding the global geopolitics
is important as well, and I understand the fact that we are in a phase of de-globalisation,
so, yeah, all of that, the broadening, all of that is, I think, very important themes
that need to be kept to close aisle.
Well, our final question, and as long-term listeners will know, I always like to insert
a little bit of a disclaimer before this one.
This is supposed to be a bit of fun and an exercise in long-term thinking.
It is, we're not actually suggesting that anybody go out there and put all of their money
on a single trade and forget about it for five years, but with that being said,
if markets were closed for five years, starting from tomorrow, and you could only make one
investment or trade before they shut, what would it be?
And you can take this anywhere, given your expertise, I don't care if it's equities or rates
